---
title: "Effects of Urban Green Spaces on Mental Health Outcomes: A Cross-Sectional Study"
author:
  - name: Jane Smith
    affiliations:
      - Department of Public Health, University of Example
  - name: John Doe
    affiliations:
      - Department of Psychology, University of Example
  - name: Maria Garcia
    affiliations:
      - Department of Urban Planning, University of Example
date: 2025-06-15
format: pdf
abstract: |
  Urban green spaces have been suggested to improve mental health,
  but evidence from diverse urban populations remains limited. This
  study examined the relationship between proximity to green spaces
  and self-reported mental health outcomes in a stratified sample of
  1,200 urban residents across three European cities. Results indicate
  a significant positive association between green space access and
  mental well-being, with effect sizes varying by age group and
  socioeconomic status.
---

## Introduction

Mental health disorders represent a growing public health concern in urban
environments worldwide. As urbanization continues to accelerate, understanding
the environmental determinants of mental health becomes increasingly important.
The World Health Organization estimates that over 55% of the global population
now lives in urban areas, a figure projected to reach 68% by 2050.

Previous research has suggested that exposure to natural environments may
have beneficial effects on psychological well-being. However, the majority
of studies have relied on cross-sectional designs with limited sample sizes,
restricting both causal inference and generalizability. A recent meta-analysis
by Chen et al. (2024) identified significant heterogeneity in effect sizes
across studies, underscoring the need for larger, multi-site investigations.

In this study, we aimed to examine the association between residential
proximity to urban green spaces and mental health outcomes in a diverse,
multi-city sample of urban residents, while also exploring potential
effect modification by sociodemographic factors.

## Methods

### Study Design and Participants

We conducted a cross-sectional survey of 1,200 adults residing in three
mid-sized European cities (Birmingham, Lyon, and Rotterdam) between
January and June 2024. Participants were recruited through stratified
random sampling of residential addresses, ensuring representation
across income quartiles. Inclusion criteria were: age 18 years or older,
residence in the study area for at least 12 months, and ability to
complete the survey in the local language or English.

### Measures

Mental health was assessed using the General Health Questionnaire
(GHQ-12), a widely used screening instrument for psychological
distress. We additionally administered the WHO-5 Well-Being Index
as a secondary outcome measure. Green space proximity was measured
as the Euclidean distance from each participant's residence to the
nearest public green space of at least 1 hectare, using municipal
GIS databases.

We also collected data on potential confounders including age,
gender, income, education level, physical activity, and neighbourhood
deprivation index.

### Statistical Analysis

We used multilevel linear regression with random intercepts for city
to examine the association between green space proximity and GHQ-12
scores, adjusting for potential confounders. We tested for effect
modification by age group and income quartile using interaction terms.
Sensitivity analyses included restricted cubic splines to assess
non-linearity. All analyses were performed using R version 4.4.0.

## Results

Of the 1,200 participants, 1,147 provided complete data and were
included in the analysis. The mean age was 44.1 years (SD = 15.3),
and 53% were female. The median distance to the nearest green space
was 340 meters (IQR: 180--620).

In the adjusted model, each 100-meter decrease in distance to
green space was associated with a 0.6-point improvement in GHQ-12
scores (β = -0.006, 95% CI: -0.010 to -0.002, p = 0.004). Results
were consistent when using the WHO-5 as the outcome measure.

The association was stronger among participants aged 60 and older
(β = -0.012, p = 0.001) compared to younger age groups. We also
observed a significant interaction with income (p = 0.02), with
stronger effects among participants in the lowest income quartile
(β = -0.011, p < 0.001).

Spline analyses suggested a non-linear relationship, with diminishing
returns beyond approximately 500 meters of proximity.

## Discussion

Our findings suggest that proximity to urban green spaces is
associated with better mental health outcomes across multiple
European cities. These results are consistent with previous research
highlighting the psychological benefits of nature exposure, and
extend the evidence base by demonstrating effect modification by
socioeconomic status.

The stronger association observed among lower-income participants
is particularly noteworthy, as these populations often have reduced
access to green spaces. This finding aligns with environmental
justice frameworks and suggests that equitable distribution of
green spaces could help reduce mental health disparities.

Several limitations should be noted. First, the cross-sectional
design precludes causal inference. Second, we relied on
self-reported mental health measures, though we mitigated this
concern by using two validated instruments. Third, we did not
account for the quality or type of green spaces, which may
influence the magnitude of health effects.

Future research should employ longitudinal designs and incorporate
objective measures of mental health, such as clinical diagnoses
from electronic health records, to strengthen the evidence base.

## Conclusion

This multi-city study provides robust evidence supporting the
mental health benefits of urban green spaces, with effects that
are particularly pronounced among older adults and lower-income
residents. Urban planners and public health practitioners should
consider these findings when designing equitable cities that
promote population well-being.
